questions.text: I find that I automatically register who is nervous in a social setting.
I believe that my emotional steadiness is something that has always been true about me, even as a child.
I trust my own judgment more than the group's consensus.
I would happily take a pay cut if it meant funding a cause I care about.
I believe that the weight of moral responsibility I carry toward those who suffer is something I actively choose and continuously renew.
I often do not realize that a conversation had multiple layers until someone explains it to me afterward.
I hold the view that real strength is the willingness to put yourself last.
I deal with problems as they come up rather than trying to anticipate every possible issue.
I believe that maintaining my own standards of living is non-negotiable.
I feel that structure provides the scaffolding on which creativity can flourish.
When I'm in a beautiful place, the experience can feel almost painfully vivid.
I believe that no one's suffering should be treated as merely an abstraction when a concrete response is possible.
I detect when a joke lands differently than the speaker intended.
I believe that attention to detail is what separates good work from great work.
I feel that being in control of a situation allows me to do my best work.
I feel that my emotional range extends further in both directions than most people's.
I have a bias toward deliberation over premature action.
I believe that the best way to honor someone's pain is to focus on what will actually help them, not on how their pain affects me.
